content.problemStatement:
Chef was relaxing outdoors after a poor performance during his university varsity's yearly week-long training retreat in the mountains. He was looking for a sign that he should continue competitive programming, even though at times, it can feel insurmountably difficult. Then, he looked up and saw a dazzling rainbow in the sky. He was so moved by the beauty of nature that he decided to continue programming and even created this problem about a mathematical Rainbow Road.

Let the Rainbow Road be a grid with MMM rows (numbered 111 through MMM from top to bottom) and NNN columns (numbered 111 through NNN from left to right). Let's denote a cell in row iii and column jjj by (i,j)(i,j)(i,j), i.e. (1,1)(1,1)(1,1) is the cell in the top left corner and (M,N)(M,N)(M,N) is the cell in the bottom right corner. Chef selected NNN distinct positive integers a1,a2,…,aNa_1, a_2, \ldots, a_Na1​,a2​,…,aN​ and instead of using colours, he "painted" stripes on the road with these numbers. For each valid jjj, each cell in column jjj has the same value aja_jaj​.

You should answer QQQ queries. In each query:


You are given an integer KKK. Chef wants to travel from the cell (1,1)(1,1)(1,1) to the cell (K,N)(K,N)(K,N).
It is only possible to travel from any cell to the cell directly below it or to the cell directly to the right (if they exist). Formally, if Chef is in a cell (i,j)(i,j)(i,j), he may only travel to (i+1,j)(i+1,j)(i+1,j) or (i,j+1)(i,j+1)(i,j+1).
Let's define a path as a sequence of cells c1,c2,…,csc_1, c_2, \ldots, c_sc1​,c2​,…,cs​ such that for each iii (1≤i<s1 \le i \lt s1≤i<s), Chef can travel from the cell cic_ici​ to the cell ci+1c_{i+1}ci+1​.
The weight of a path is the product of the values of all cells in that path.
Chef wants you to find the sum of the weights of all possible paths that start in the cell (1,1)(1,1)(1,1) and end in the cell (K,N)(K,N)(K,N). Since this number can be quite large, compute it modulo 998,244,353=7⋅17⋅223+1998,244,353 = 7 \cdot 17 \cdot 2^{23}+1998,244,353=7⋅17⋅223+1 (a prime number).
constraints: a1,a2,…,aNa_1, a_2, \ldots, a_Na1​,a2​,…,aN​ are pairwise distinct

`ExplanationIn the first query, there is only one path from (1,1)(1,1)(1,1) to (1,3)(1,3)(1,3): (1,1)→(1,2)→(1,3)(1,1) \rightarrow (1,2) \rightarrow (1,3)(1,1)→(1,2)→(1,3). This path has weight 1⋅3⋅5=151 \cdot 3 \cdot 5 = 151⋅3⋅5=15.
In the second query, there are three paths from (1,1)(1,1)(1,1) to (2,3)(2,3)(2,3):
`ExplanationIn the last query, there are five paths from (1,1)(1,1)(1,1) to (5,2)(5,2)(5,2). Their weights are 222, 444, 888, 161616 and 323232, which sum up to 626262.
content.inputFormat:
The first line of the input contains three space-separated integers MMM, NNN and QQQ.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers a1,a2,…,aNa_1, a_2, \ldots, a_Na1​,a2​,…,aN​.
QQQ lines follow. Each of these lines contains a single integer KKK describing a query.
content.outputFormat: For each query, print a single line containing one integer ― the sum of weights of paths modulo 998,244,353998,244,353998,244,353.
